title: The Corpus Hermeticum reaches Florence
blurb: Cosimo de' Medici had Ficino drop his Plato to translate a newly-arrived Greek manuscript of Hermes Trismegistus first — such was the prestige of the 'ancient theology.'
source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. corpus hermeticum

title: Ficino and the prisca theologia
blurb: Marsilio Ficino argued for a single ancient wisdom running from Hermes and Zoroaster through Plato to Christ — the prisca theologia that made pagan magic safe to study.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. prisca theologia, philosophia perennis

title: Ficino's De vita makes talisman-magic philosophy
blurb: The third book of De vita coelitus comparanda turned drawing down planetary spiritus into respectable Neoplatonic natural philosophy, giving the astral talisman an intellectual pedigree.
mtg: Every 'Talisman' mana-rock is this idea in miniature — a made object that channels cosmic power.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. spiritus mundi, anima mundi; Copenhaver

title: Pico's 900 Theses and Christian Kabbalah
blurb: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola proposed that magic and Kabbalah, rightly used, prove Christ's divinity — inventing Christian Cabala and the dignity of the magus-philosopher.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.vv. cabala, dignitas hominis; Yates

title: Reuchlin's De Verbo Mirifico
blurb: Johann Reuchlin brought Hebrew letter-mysticism into Christian scholarship, arguing that the divine Name itself was the 'wonder-working word.'

title: Trithemius' Steganographia
blurb: Abbot Johannes Trithemius wrote a book that looked like angel-magic and was partly cryptography — the ancestor of both the grimoire and the cipher, and a scandal for centuries.

title: Agrippa's De Occulta Philosophia
blurb: Heinrich Cornelius Agrippa's three books synthesised natural, celestial, and ceremonial magic into the Renaissance encyclopaedia of the occult — the single most influential magic book ever printed.
mtg: The master-source that fixed the vocabulary of learned magic our lexicon still tracks.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. de occulta philosophia; AgrippaDOP

title: Paracelsus reinvents alchemy as medicine
blurb: Theophrastus von Hohenheim, 'Paracelsus,' turned alchemy from gold-making toward healing — the spagyric art of extracting the virtues of plants and minerals into medicines.

title: Agrippa's magic reaches print
blurb: Printed in full, the De Occulta Philosophia put a complete system of magic — planetary, angelic, cabalistic — into any literate hand, spreading the arcana across Europe.

title: The Malleus Maleficarum
blurb: Kramer's witch-hunting manual fused maleficium (harmful magic), heresy, and a misogynist demonology into a template that helped power two centuries of trials.
mtg: The dark inversion — magic reframed as a capital crime.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. maleficium; Bailey, The Sacred and the Sinister

title: Emblem books are born
blurb: Alciato's Emblemata launched the emblem — a motto, a picture, and a verse combining into a compressed symbol — the form later alchemists like Maier would use to encode the Work.
mtg: The card as emblem: image, name, and text making one meaning.

title: Della Porta's Magia Naturalis
blurb: Giambattista della Porta catalogued 'natural magic' — the marvels achievable by knowing nature's secret sympathies — pushing magic toward what would become experimental science.
source: RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. magia naturalis; William Eamon (alchemy corpus)

title: Weyer argues the witches are ill, not guilty
blurb: Johann Weyer's De Praestigiis Daemonum contended that accused witches were deluded melancholics, not diabolists — an early, brave blow against the trials.
source: Stuart Clark, Thinking with Demons (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Dee's Monas Hieroglyphica
blurb: John Dee condensed the whole cosmos into a single glyph, claiming it unlocked alchemy, astronomy, and the Kabbalah at once — the ultimate sigil.

title: Dee and Kelley's angelic conversations
blurb: Scrying into a crystal, John Dee and Edward Kelley recorded a whole angelic language (Enochian) and built the Sigillum Dei Aemeth, a great circular talisman, from dictated instructions.
mtg: The scrying mirror and the seal — divination and talisman together at magic's Renaissance summit.
source: Deborah Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels (renaissance magic corpus); RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. sigillum dei aemeth

title: Scot's Discoverie of Witchcraft
blurb: Reginald Scot debunked witchcraft as fraud and conjuring-tricks — and in doing so printed real conjuring methods, becoming an accidental grimoire.
source: Keith Thomas, Religion and the Decline of Magic (renaissance magic corpus)

title: Giordano Bruno's art of memory and magic
blurb: Bruno fused Hermetic magic with the classical art of memory, building interior temples of images to grasp the cosmos — and was burned in 1600 for his pains.
source: Yates, Giordano Bruno and the Hermetic Tradition; RenaissanceMagicDB, s.v. ars memoriae

title: Khunrath's Amphitheatrum
blurb: Heinrich Khunrath's engraved 'oratory-laboratory' fused prayer and alchemy in one image — the magus at once on his knees and at the furnace.
source: Peter Forshaw, The Mage's Images (alchemy corpus)
